1.synthesis:
Psalm 71 is an anonymous psalm widely understood as an old man's prayer based on vv. 9 and 18 ('when old age comes,' 'in old age and gray hair'). It is heavily intertextual, weaving together language from Ps 22 (birth-covenant), Ps 31 (rock of refuge), and the pattern of lament-to-praise. The opening line quotes Ps 31:1 nearly verbatim: 'In you, O LORD, I take refuge; let me never be put to shame' — establishing the psalm as a consciously composed meditation on earlier Psalter tradition. The psalmist is one who has been formed by the liturgical texts of the community and prays out of that formation.

The first section is a cluster of petitions grounded in covenant confidence: 'be for me a sheltering rock, a place I can always come to' (v. 3). The specific phrase 'a place I can always come to (bêt mĕṣûdôt)' implies habitual return — this is not a one-time rescue but a lifetime shelter sought repeatedly. 'You have commanded my salvation' (v. 3) is a striking claim: YHWH has decreed the psalmist's salvation as a sovereign act. The praise vow of vv. 6–8 — 'upon you I have leaned from before my birth; my praise is continually of you' — grounds the lifelong covenant relationship in pre-birth divine sustaining: the God who held the psalmist from birth continues to be the one depended upon in old age.
1.historical_context: The intertextual nature of Psalm 71 — drawing on Pss 22, 31, and other earlier Psalter material — reflects a Second Temple practice of anthologizing and meditating on the Psalter as scripture: the psalmist does not merely quote but weaves earlier texts into a new prayer, showing that the Psalter was being used as the prayer-book of the covenant community. The appeal to YHWH's covenant righteousness (ṣidqāh, v. 2) as the basis for deliverance grounds the prayer in YHWH's own character rather than the psalmist's merit. The 'sheltering rock (sûr mĕʿônāh)' combines the geological metaphor of Judean limestone geography with the theological metaphor of divine refuge used throughout the Psalter (Ps 18:2; Ps 31:2–3; Ps 62:2).
1.literary_structure: The first section (vv. 1–8) establishes the covenant relationship's historical depth: refuge in YHWH from youth, trust since before birth, lifelong praise. This temporal depth is the theological foundation for the old man's prayer in the sections that follow: what he asks for in old age is what he has known throughout life. The movement from petition (vv. 1–4) to confident testimony (vv. 5–8) mirrors the lament-to-confidence structure of individual psalms, here telescoped into the first section.
1.theological_application: The psalm's grounding of prayer in lifelong covenant relationship models the accumulated trust that makes old age's prayer different from young faith's petition: the elder who prays 'in you I take refuge' does so out of decades of experienced divine faithfulness. The testimony 'my praise is continually of you' (v. 6) is the fruit of a life shaped by regular covenant worship. This is the Psalter's vision of sanctification: not dramatic encounters but the steady accumulation of covenant faithfulness expressed in praise and trust, forming a person who can face old age's vulnerabilities with the same foundation they have always had.
1.christology: The pre-birth covenant relationship of v. 6 — 'upon you I have leaned from before my birth; you are he who took me from my mother's womb' — reflects the prophetic self-understanding of being known before formation (Jer 1:5; Isa 49:1). This reaches its fullest expression in Christ, the eternal Son who took on flesh — the one who was 'leaned upon' from before birth in the fullest sense, being himself the Word through whom all things were created (John 1:1–3). The habitual shelter of v. 3 — 'a place I can always come to' — is the invitation of Christ's 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28).

9.pericope_label: Do Not Cast Me Away in Old Age
9.synthesis:
The second section of Psalm 71 is the specific prayer that identifies this as an old man's prayer: 'Do not cast me off in the time of old age; forsake me not when my strength is spent' (v. 9). The vulnerability of age — diminished strength, increased social and physical vulnerability — combined with the specific threat of enemies who 'see that God has forsaken me' and conspire against the weakened one (vv. 10–11) creates the psalm's particular poignancy. The fear is not merely physical weakness but the theological threat: that the visible diminishment of old age might be read as evidence of divine abandonment. The enemies' counsel — 'pursue and seize him, for there is no one to deliver him' — capitalizes on this apparent vulnerability.

The petition of vv. 12–13 — 'O God, be not far from me; O my God, make haste to help me! May my accusers be put to shame and consumed' — is urgent precisely because the old man's physical resources for self-defense are diminished. Everything now depends on divine intervention. This is the psalm's theological argument: old age strips away all the resources that might have once been available, leaving only God, which is exactly the condition from which the psalm began — 'in you I take refuge.'
9.historical_context: Old age in the ancient Near East was simultaneously honored and vulnerable: the elder commanded respect but also faced diminished economic and physical capacity without the protections of the household system. The specific threat of enemies who 'see that God has forsaken him' reflects the ancient theology of divine favor visible in circumstances: prosperity was interpreted as divine blessing, suffering as divine withdrawal. The psalm pushes back against this theology: apparent divine abandonment (visible in old age's weakness and the enemies' power) does not mean actual divine abandonment. The covenant relationship persists through circumstances that might be misread as evidence of its end.
9.literary_structure: The section's brevity (five verses) and urgency model prayer in extremity: the petition is compressed and direct, lacking the extended elaboration of the first section. This compression is appropriate to the urgency: the enemies are already conspiring, the strength is already spent, the appeal to divine haste is as urgent as Psalm 70's ḥûšāh. The section functions as the psalm's crisis point, making the confident praise of the next two sections (vv. 14–24) all the more theologically charged: the praise is not despite the crisis but through it, because YHWH has not in fact abandoned the old man.
9.theological_application: The prayer 'do not cast me off in old age' addresses the specific pastoral situation of the elderly believer who faces diminishment with the accompanying theological question: does God's faithfulness extend through the decline of all the capacities that once seemed to be the instruments of covenant service? The psalm's answer is implicitly yes: the same God who sustained from before birth sustains through old age's weakness, and the diminishment that strips away human capacity only makes more visible the divine sustaining that was always the actual ground of the life.
9.christology: The prayer against being cast off in weakness resonates with Christ's cry of dereliction — 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matt 27:46) — the ultimate experience of divine 'casting off' that the psalmist fears. But Christ was genuinely forsaken so that the psalmist need not be: the divine abandonment the cross enacted is the permanent removing of the threat of divine abandonment from all who are in Christ. 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' (Heb 13:5) is the NT's answer to the old man's prayer — grounded in the cross where the forsaking happened once, fully, to the One, so it need not happen to those who are his.

14.synthesis:
The third section of Psalm 71 is brief but theologically pivotal — three verses that commit the psalmist to perpetual praise grounded in lifelong divine faithfulness: 'But I will hope continually and will praise you yet more and more' (v. 14). The escalating praise — 'yet more and more' (wĕhôsaptî ʿal kol-tĕhillatekā, I will add to all your praise) — reflects the logic that each new experience of divine faithfulness increases rather than exhausts the material for praise. A lifetime of divine rescue is not a diminishing resource for thanksgiving but an expanding one: more and more has been given to praise.

The testimony resolution of vv. 15–16 — 'My mouth will tell of your righteous acts, of your deeds of salvation all the day, for their number is past my knowledge. With the mighty deeds of the Lord GOD I will come' — acknowledges the inexhaustibility of divine acts worth recounting. 'Their number is past my knowledge' (lōʾ yādaʿtî sĕpōrôt) is the admission of the incompleteness of the testimony that follows: no matter how long the praise continues, the acts of divine righteousness exceed what can be counted or recounted. This is not despair but the doxological awe before an inexhaustible subject.
14.historical_context: The commitment to praise 'all the day' (kol hayyôm) reflects the multiple-daily-prayer discipline of the Psalter and Second Temple piety: praise as a sustained orientation of the day rather than isolated moments. 'Your righteousness, and your salvation only' (v. 15) establishes the content of the praise: the ṣidqāh (righteousness) and yĕšûʿāh (salvation) that are specifically divine are the subjects. The phrase 'I will come with the mighty deeds of the Lord GOD' (v. 16) — approaching the sanctuary in procession with recitation of divine deeds as the processional content — reflects the cultic practice of testimony as part of formal worship.
14.literary_structure: This brief section functions as the pivot between the crisis-petition (vv. 9–13) and the extended testimony (vv. 17–24). The commitment to 'hope continually' (wāʾănî tāmîd ʾăyaḥēl) is the theological resolution of the crisis: not a claim that the circumstances have improved but a commitment of the will to sustained hope and praise regardless of circumstances. The escalating praise structure — 'yet more and more' — opposes the logic that old age's limitations diminish the capacity for praise; instead, the accumulated experience of divine faithfulness increases it.
14.theological_application: The principle of escalating praise — that each act of divine faithfulness adds to rather than exhausts the material for praise — grounds the spiritual discipline of gratitude as a renewable and expanding resource. The older one is in Christ, the more material for praise has accumulated; the testimony that began in youth becomes richer and more comprehensive in old age. The commitment to 'praise more and more' is the counter-movement to the age-related temptation toward bitterness, withdrawal, or diminished expectation: praise is the posture that keeps the covenant relationship active and life-giving through all stages of life.
14.christology: The inexhaustible acts of divine righteousness and salvation — 'their number is past my knowledge' (v. 15) — reach their fullest expression in Christ, in whom 'all the promises of God are Yes' (2 Cor 1:20). The praise that mounts 'more and more' through a lifetime of covenant experience is the movement toward the eternal praise of the new creation, where the inexhaustibility of the divine acts of salvation fills an eternity without diminishment. 'Lord GOD' (Adonai Yhwh, v. 16) — the doubled covenant name — is the name above all names that Christ bears in his exaltation (Phil 2:9–11).

17.synthesis:
The final section of Psalm 71 is the extended testimony that has been building through the psalm — the old man's comprehensive witness to YHWH's teaching through a lifetime. 'O God, from my youth you have taught me, and I still proclaim your wondrous deeds' (v. 17) — the divine teaching that began in youth continues in old age; the psalmist is still being formed and still proclaiming. The central petition of the section — 'so even to old age and gray hair, O God, do not forsake me, until I proclaim your might to another generation, your power to all those to come' (v. 18) — grounds the request for longevity in inter-generational testimony: the old man wants to live long enough to pass on what he has learned about YHWH.

The section climaxes in extended praise (vv. 19–24): YHWH's righteousness 'reaches the high heavens' (v. 19), the 'great things' and 'troubles many and bitter' (v. 20) that YHWH has shown and through which he will restore — 'you will revive me again; from the depths of the earth you will bring me up again' (v. 20). The lyre-and-harp praise of v. 22, the vocal praise of v. 23, and the tongue's meditating on righteousness all day (v. 24) form a comprehensive picture of the old man's total life of praise — instrument, voice, and thought all oriented toward the God who has been faithful from before birth through old age.
17.historical_context: Inter-generational testimony was a central covenant obligation: 'Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children, and their children to another generation' (Joel 1:3; cf. Deut 6:20–25; Ps 78:1–8). The old man's prayer to live long enough to transmit what he has learned grounds the request for longevity not in self-interest but in covenant obligation. The 'depths of the earth' resurrection language (v. 20) uses the standard ANE image of Sheol as the underworld beneath the earth — YHWH's raising from the depths is the restoration from extremity, though later tradition reads this as anticipating literal resurrection (Ezek 37:12).
17.literary_structure: The final section resolves the psalm's sustained tension between old age's vulnerability and lifelong covenant faithfulness in the direction of praise. The structure is: lifelong teaching received and still proclaimed (v. 17), petition for continuation for inter-generational testimony (v. 18), praise of divine righteousness reaching to heaven (v. 19), recollection of troubles-and-restoration (v. 20), renewed praise-vow (vv. 22–24). The psalm ends as it began — with praise — but the praise at the end is richer, more explicit, and more comprehensive than the promise at the beginning.
17.theological_application: The inter-generational testimony motive — 'do not forsake me until I can tell the next generation' (v. 18) — grounds the old believer's longing for life in others' spiritual need rather than in personal reluctance to die. The old man does not pray for longevity as an end in itself but as a means of transmitting the knowledge of divine faithfulness that he has accumulated. This is the church's elder theology: those who have lived through the covenant community's difficulties possess irreplaceable experiential knowledge of divine faithfulness that must be transmitted before it is lost. The church that does not have the wisdom of its elders accessible is impoverished.
17.christology: The resurrection language of v. 20 — 'from the depths of the earth you will bring me up again' — is the OT anticipation of what YHWH did for Christ and through Christ will do for all who are in him. 'I will restore your fortunes and comfort you again' (v. 21) is the promise that resurrection constitutes: the comfort after great trouble, the restoration after the depths, the increase of honor after humiliation. The lyre-and-harp praise of v. 22 — praising 'the Holy One of Israel' — is the music of the community that recognizes that YHWH's faithfulness in Christ has delivered on every promise that the old man trusted across a lifetime.
